does: This paper introduces Gemma Scope 2, a comprehensive set of open-source interpretability tools designed to provide fine-grained access to the internal representations and behaviors of language models across the entire Gemma 3 family. The work releases dictionary learning artifacts, including sparse autoencoders trained on hidden states and activations, that decompose the complex high-dimensional activations of language models into interpretable features. These tools enable researchers to identify and analyze what individual neurons and feature combinations compute, trace how information flows through model layers, and understand the mechanisms behind specific model behaviors, from basic linguistic phenomena to more complex reasoning patterns.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to reverse-engineer neural network decision-making, extending prior work on dictionary learning and sparse autoencoders as techniques for feature extraction and analysis. It responds to increasing demand within the AI safety community for interpretable models and transparent internal representations, following earlier efforts to provide interpretability access for smaller models and closed-source systems. The release complements and extends previous interpretability frameworks by offering comprehensive coverage across an entire model family at scale, democratizing access to the kinds of detailed mechanistic insights previously available only for research toy models or limited proprietary systems.
enables: The availability of these interpretability tools across Gemma 3 enables systematic safety research investigating how models develop problematic behaviors, implement deceptive reasoning, or exhibit alignment failures—work that requires seeing inside the model to understand causation rather than just observing outputs. Researchers can now systematically map circuits implementing specific capabilities and vulnerabilities, test interventions to redirect or remove unwanted behaviors, and develop scalable oversight techniques that leverage interpretability for monitoring and controlling large language models. This infrastructure supports the development of more targeted training procedures, better understanding of emergent properties in scaling, and ultimately contributes to building AI systems whose behavior can be deeply understood and reliably controlled.
